lessons.title: Default constructors and default arguments

exercises.title: Inventory Item with Default Constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Item` class so it can be constructed with no arguments, with only a name, or with both a name and a quantity.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 20), the number of items to read.
- Lines 2..N+1: each line contains either:
  - the single word `DEFAULT` (construct with no arguments),
  - one string with no spaces (construct with name only, quantity defaults to 1),
  - or one string followed by one integer (construct with name and quantity).

## Output
For each item, print one line in the format `name: qty` where `name` is the item's name and `qty` is its quantity.
- When constructed with no arguments, name must be `unknown` and quantity must be `0`.
- When constructed with name only, quantity must be `1`.

## Requirements
1. Define a single constructor `Item(std::string name = "unknown", int qty = 0)` that serves as both the default constructor and the parameterized constructor — do **not** define a separate `Item()` overload.
2. The default quantity for the one-argument form (name only) must be `1`, not `0`. Achieve this by giving `qty` a default value of `1` in a second constructor — actually, use **two separate constructors**: one `Item() = default` that sets `name="unknown"` and `qty=0` via in-class member initializers, and one `Item(std::string name, int qty = 1)` for the parameterized form.
3. Provide `getName()` and `getQty()` const member functions.
4. Input lines starting with `DEFAULT` must use the zero-argument construction path (i.e., `Item{}`), producing `unknown: 0`.
5. Output lines use the exact format `name: qty` with no extra spaces.

## Notes
- Use `std::string` for the name member.
- Read the line type by peeking at the first token: if it equals `"DEFAULT"`, construct with no arguments; if followed by an integer, construct with both; otherwise construct with name only.

test_cases.label: Hidden: name-only and full construction edge cases

exercises.title: Temperature Log with Default-Argument Constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `TempReading` class and write a program that stores a sequence of temperature readings, then prints a summary using each object's member functions.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 50), the number of readings.
- Lines 2..N+1: each line is one of:
  - `DEFAULT` — use a zero-argument construction (sensor id = 0, celsius = 0.0).
  - an integer S — construction with sensor id S only (celsius defaults to 20.0).
  - an integer S followed by a double T — construction with sensor id S and celsius T.

## Output
For each reading, print one line:
`Sensor S: T C` where S is the sensor id and T is the celsius value printed to **one decimal place**.

Example line: `Sensor 3: 36.6 C`

## Requirements
1. Define a **single constructor** `TempReading(int sensorId = 0, double celsius = 20.0)` using a member initializer list. This one constructor must handle all three construction forms — do **not** use `= default` or a separate no-argument overload.
2. Confirm that `TempReading{}` compiles and produces sensorId = 0, celsius = 20.0 (because all parameters have defaults, this constructor *is* the default constructor).
3. Provide `getSensorId()` and `getCelsius()` const member functions.
4. Output uses `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)`.
5. When a line contains only an integer (no double following it), celsius must default to `20.0`. When the line is `DEFAULT`, both must use their defaults (0 and 20.0).
6. Hidden tests include: all `DEFAULT` lines, all three-token lines with negative temperatures, and a single reading with N = 1.

## Notes
- Include `<iomanip>` for `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision`.
- Read the line type by checking whether the first token is the string `"DEFAULT"`; otherwise parse it as an integer and then optionally as a double.

test_cases.label: Sample: mixed input forms

Sensor 7: 36.6 C

Sensor 2: 20.0 C

Hidden: negative temps and large sensor ids

Sensor 0: 20.0 C

Sensor 99: 20.0 C

lessons.title: Delegating constructors

exercises.title: Book Constructor Chain
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Book` struct so that its three constructors form a delegation chain, each supplying default values for omitted arguments.

## Input
- Line 1: a string (the book title, no spaces)
- Line 2: a string (the author name, no spaces)
- Line 3: an integer (the page count, 1 ≤ pages ≤ 10000)

Three separate `Book` objects are constructed and printed:
1. Using all three values (title, author, pages)
2. Using only title and author (pages defaults to 300)
3. Using only title (author defaults to `"Unknown"`, pages defaults to 300)

## Output
Print exactly 3 lines, each in the format:
```
<title> by <author>, <pages> pages
```
Line 1 uses all three input values. Line 2 uses the input title and author with 300 pages. Line 3 uses only the input title with author `Unknown` and 300 pages.

## Requirements
1. Define `struct Book` with string fields `title` and `author`, and int field `pages`.
2. Implement a three-parameter constructor `Book(std::string title, std::string author, int pages)` that directly initializes all three members.
3. Implement a two-parameter constructor `Book(std::string title, std::string author)` that **delegates** to the three-parameter constructor, passing `300` for pages.
4. Implement a one-parameter constructor `explicit Book(std::string title)` that **delegates** to the two-parameter constructor, passing `"Unknown"` for author.
5. No constructor may initialize members directly except the three-parameter constructor — the delegating constructors must use only a delegation call in their initializer lists.
6. The `main()` provided in the starter code must not be modified.

test_cases.label: Sample: all three provided
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dune by Herbert, 412 pages
Dune by Herbert, 300 pages
Dune by Unknown, 300 pages

test_cases.label: Hidden: single-word title different author
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Neuromancer by Gibson, 271 pages
Neuromancer by Gibson, 300 pages
Neuromancer by Unknown, 300 pages

test_cases.label: Hidden: pages = 1 edge case
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Haiku by Bashō, 1 pages
Haiku by Bashō, 300 pages
Haiku by Unknown, 300 pages

exercises.title: Temperature Sensor with Delegation and Member Functions
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Sensor` struct, which uses delegating constructors and a member function to report its state.

## Input
- Line 1: a string (sensor name, no spaces)
- Line 2: a double (initial temperature reading in Celsius, -100.0 ≤ t ≤ 500.0)
- Line 3: an integer (sample count, 1 ≤ count ≤ 1000)

Four `Sensor` objects are constructed and their `report()` output is printed:
1. `Sensor` built with name, temperature, and sample count (all three input values)
2. `Sensor` built with name and temperature (sample count defaults to 1)
3. `Sensor` built with name only (temperature defaults to 0.0, sample count defaults to 1)
4. `Sensor` built with no arguments (name defaults to `"default"`, temperature defaults to 0.0, sample count defaults to 1)

## Output
Print exactly 4 lines. Each line is produced by calling `report()` on the respective object. The format is:
```
Sensor <name>: <temperature>C (<count> sample(s))
```
Temperature must be printed with exactly 1 decimal place. Use the word `sample(s)` literally (not pluralized conditionally).

Example for name=`outdoor`, temperature=`23.5`, count=`10`:
```
Sensor outdoor: 23.5C (10 sample(s))
```

## Requirements
1. Define `struct Sensor` with fields: `std::string name`, `double temperature`, `int count`.
2. Implement a three-parameter constructor `Sensor(std::string name, double temperature, int count)` that directly initializes all three members.
3. Implement a two-parameter constructor `Sensor(std::string name, double temperature)` that **delegates** to the three-parameter constructor with `count = 1`.
4. Implement a one-parameter constructor `explicit Sensor(std::string name)` that **delegates** to the two-parameter constructor with `temperature = 0.0`.
5. Implement a zero-parameter (default) constructor `Sensor()` that **delegates** to the one-parameter constructor with `name = "default"`.
6. Implement a `void report() const` member function that prints one line in the exact format above (1 decimal place for temperature).
7. No delegating constructor may initialize members directly — only the three-parameter constructor does so.
8. The `main()` provided in the starter code must not be modified.

Sensor default: 0.0C (1 sample(s))

Sensor freezer: -18.0C (1 sample(s))
Sensor freezer: 0.0C (1 sample(s))
Sensor default: 0.0C (1 sample(s))

lessons.title: Temporary class objects

exercises.title: Print Rectangle Area and Perimeter via Temporaries
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Rectangle` struct and a free function `print_stats` so that the program constructs temporary `Rectangle` objects, calls member functions on them directly, and prints area and perimeter — all without storing a named variable.

## Input
- Line 1: two positive integers `W` and `H` (1 ≤ W, H ≤ 1000), separated by a space — the width and height of a rectangle.

## Output
Print exactly two lines:
- Line 1: `Area: X` where X is the integer area (W * H).
- Line 2: `Perimeter: Y` where Y is the integer perimeter (2 * (W + H)).

No trailing blank lines. No extra spaces.

## Requirements
1. Define a struct `Rectangle` with integer member fields `width` and `height`, both zero-initialized by default.
2. `Rectangle` must have a constructor that accepts two `int` arguments (width, height) and initializes the fields via a member initializer list.
3. Add a `const` member function `area()` returning `int` (product of width and height).
4. Add a `const` member function `perimeter()` returning `int` (2 * (width + height)).
5. Define a free function `void print_stats(Rectangle r)` that prints the two output lines using `r.area()` and `r.perimeter()`.
6. In `main`, call `print_stats` with a **temporary** `Rectangle` object constructed from the input values — do **not** declare a named `Rectangle` variable in `main`.
7. Also call `.area()` on a temporary inline (the result may be discarded) to demonstrate direct member-function chaining on a temporary — this call must appear in `main` but does not affect output.
8. Edge cases covered by hidden tests: W = 1 H = 1, W = 1000 H = 1000.

exercises.title: Closest Point to Origin Using Temporaries
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Point` struct and a free function `closest_to_origin` so the program reads a list of points, builds temporary `Point` objects, and returns the one with the smallest distance from the origin.

## Input
- Line 1: integer `N` (1 ≤ N ≤ 100) — the number of points.
- Lines 2..N+1: two integers `X` and `Y` (-1000 ≤ X, Y ≤ 1000) separated by a space, one point per line.

## Output
Print exactly one line: `Closest: (X, Y)` where X and Y are the coordinates of the point nearest to the origin (0, 0) by Euclidean distance. No trailing blank lines. No extra spaces.

If two points are equidistant from the origin, print the one that appears **first** in the input.

## Requirements
1. Define a struct `Point` with integer member fields `x` and `y`, both zero-initialized by default.
2. `Point` must have a constructor taking two `int` arguments that initializes `x` and `y` via a member initializer list.
3. Add a `const` member function `dist_sq()` returning `int` — the squared Euclidean distance from the origin (x*x + y*y). Using squared distance avoids floating-point and is sufficient for comparison.
4. Define a free function `Point closest_to_origin(Point a, Point b)` that returns the point with the smaller `dist_sq()`; if equal, return `a`.
5. In `main`, read all N coordinate pairs. For each pair, construct a **temporary** `Point{x, y}` and pass it directly to `closest_to_origin` — do not store named `Point` variables inside the reading loop.
6. Accumulate the result of each `closest_to_origin` call in a single named `Point` variable declared before the loop (initialized to the first point); the loop processes points 2..N by passing temporaries.
7. Print the result in the exact format `Closest: (X, Y)`.
8. Edge cases covered by hidden tests: N = 1 (single point), a point at the origin (0 0), negative coordinates, two points equidistant from origin.

lessons.title: Introduction to the copy constructor

exercises.title: Trace Copy Constructor Calls for a Temperature Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Temperature` class so that its custom copy constructor prints a trace message each time an object is copied, then write two helper functions that trigger copies so the trace can be observed.

## Input
- Line 1: a single floating-point number representing a temperature in Celsius (e.g. `36.6`).

## Output
Print exactly the following lines in order (replace `<value>` with the Celsius value read, formatted to 1 decimal place):

```
Constructed: <value>C
Copy constructor: <value>C
Copy constructor: <value>C
Passed by value: <value>C
Copy constructor: <value>C
Returned by value: <value>C
```

- Line 1: printed by the regular constructor when the first object is built.
- Line 2: printed by the copy constructor when `b` is direct-initialized from `a`.
- Line 3: printed by the copy constructor when `printByValue` receives its argument.
- Line 4: printed inside `printByValue` to confirm the value received.
- Line 5: printed by the copy constructor when `makeTemperature` copies its local object into the caller. (Note: if copy elision fires here your compiler may omit this line — see the Notes section.)
- Line 6: printed inside `main` to show the returned object's value.

## Requirements
1. `Temperature` must have a `double celsius` data member.
2. The regular constructor `Temperature(double c)` must print `Constructed: <value>C` (1 decimal place).
3. The copy constructor signature must be `Temperature(const Temperature& src)` and must print `Copy constructor: <value>C` (1 decimal place) using the *new* object's value after copying.
4. Implement `void printByValue(Temperature t)` — it receives by value (triggering the copy constructor) and prints `Passed by value: <value>C`.
5. Implement `Temperature makeTemperature(double c)` — constructs a local `Temperature` and returns it by value.
6. In `main`: read one `double`, construct `Temperature a{val}`, then copy-initialize `Temperature b = a`, call `printByValue(b)`, then call `makeTemperature(val)` and print the returned object's celsius to 1 decimal place prefixed with `Returned by value: `.
7. All floating-point output must use exactly 1 decimal place (use `std::format` or `std::fixed` + `std::setprecision(1)`).
8. Compile and run with `-fno-elide-constructors` to ensure the copy for the return value is not elided (the grader uses this flag).

## Notes
- You may use `#include <iostream>`, `#include <format>`, and `#include <iomanip>`.
- The grader compiles with `-std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra -fno-elide-constructors`.

test_cases.label: Sample: 36.6°C
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed: 36.6C
Copy constructor: 36.6C
Copy constructor: 36.6C
Passed by value: 36.6C
Constructed: 36.6C
Copy constructor: 36.6C
Returned by value: 36.6C

test_cases.label: Hidden: 0.0°C (edge — zero value)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed: 0.0C
Copy constructor: 0.0C
Copy constructor: 0.0C
Passed by value: 0.0C
Constructed: 0.0C
Copy constructor: 0.0C
Returned by value: 0.0C

test_cases.label: Hidden: -10.5°C (negative value)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed: -10.5C
Copy constructor: -10.5C
Copy constructor: -10.5C
Passed by value: -10.5C
Constructed: -10.5C
Copy constructor: -10.5C
Returned by value: -10.5C

exercises.title: Student Grade Book with Copy Constructor and Const Methods
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Student` class, which stores a name and an array of up to 5 integer grades. The class must use a custom copy constructor and expose `const`-qualified methods for reading data — combining copy-constructor knowledge with const member functions.

## Input
- Line 1: the student's name (a single word, no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 5), the number of grades.
- Lines 3 .. N+2: one integer grade per line (0 ≤ grade ≤ 100).

## Output
Print exactly 6 lines:

```
Original: <name> avg=<average>
Copy constructor called for: <name>
Copy: <name> avg=<average>
After rename, original: <original_name> avg=<average>
After rename, copy: <new_name> avg=<average>
Grades match: <yes|no>
```

- `<average>` is the average of the N grades, formatted to 2 decimal places.
- Line 2 is printed inside the copy constructor itself when it copies the object.
- `<new_name>` is the copy's name after calling `rename("Clone")` on the copy only.
- `<original_name>` is the original's name, which must be unchanged.
- The last line prints `yes` if every grade in the copy equals the corresponding grade in the original (same indices, same count), otherwise `no`.

## Requirements
1. `Student` must have these private data members: `std::string name`, `int grades[5]`, `int count`.
2. Provide a constructor `Student(const std::string& name, const int* g, int n)` that stores the name, copies the first `n` values from `g` into `grades`, and sets `count = n`.
3. The copy constructor `Student(const Student& src)` must perform memberwise deep copy of all three members and print `Copy constructor called for: <name>` using the *source* object's name before copying.
4. Provide a `const` method `double average() const` that returns the mean of the stored grades.
5. Provide a `const` method `const std::string& getName() const` that returns the name.
6. Provide a `const` method `int getGrade(int i) const` that returns `grades[i]`.
7. Provide a non-const method `void rename(const std::string& newName)` that changes only the name.
8. In `main`: read the input, build `Student original`, copy it into `Student copy = original`, call `copy.rename("Clone")`, then print the 6 output lines using the const methods above.
9. All averages must be printed with exactly 2 decimal places.
10. The `Grades match` check must compare every grade index from 0 to count-1.

## Notes
- You may use `#include <iostream>`, `#include <string>`, `#include <format>`, and `#include <iomanip>`.
- Only `const` methods may be called on a `const Student&` — ensure your accessor signatures are correct or the grader's `-Wall -Wextra` build will warn/error.

test_cases.label: Sample: Alice, 3 grades

Copy constructor called for: Alice
Original: Alice avg=80.00
Copy: Clone avg=80.00
After rename, original: Alice avg=80.00
After rename, copy: Clone avg=80.00
Grades match: yes

test_cases.label: Hidden: Bob, 1 grade (edge — single grade)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Copy constructor called for: Bob
Original: Bob avg=55.00
Copy: Clone avg=55.00
After rename, original: Bob avg=55.00
After rename, copy: Clone avg=55.00
Grades match: yes

test_cases.label: Hidden: Zara, 5 grades (maximum count, non-round average)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Copy constructor called for: Zara
Original: Zara avg=84.00
Copy: Clone avg=84.00
After rename, original: Zara avg=84.00
After rename, copy: Clone avg=84.00
Grades match: yes

lessons.title: Class initialization and copy elision

exercises.title: Track Constructor vs Copy Constructor Calls
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Sensor` class so the program counts how many times the constructor and copy constructor are each called, then prints those counts after a series of initializations.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10), the number of "copy from lvalue" operations to perform after the initial setup.

## Output
Print exactly two lines:
```
Constructor calls: C
Copy constructor calls: X
```
where `C` and `X` are the actual counts accumulated during the program (see Requirements for which initializations are performed).

## Requirements
1. Define `struct Sensor` with a `std::string label` field and two static `int` counters: `ctor_count` and `copy_count`, both initialized to `0`.
2. The converting constructor `Sensor(std::string lbl)` must increment `ctor_count` and store `lbl`.
3. The copy constructor `Sensor(const Sensor&)` must increment `copy_count` and copy `label`.
4. Before reading N, perform these three initializations in order:
   - `Sensor a{"alpha"};` — direct initialization
   - `Sensor b = make_sensor();` — where `make_sensor()` returns `Sensor{"beta"}` by value (mandatory elision applies; only the constructor fires)
   - `Sensor c = a;` — copy-initialization from lvalue; copy constructor fires
5. After reading N, perform exactly N copy-initializations of the form `Sensor tmp = a;` in a loop (each fires the copy constructor).
6. Print the two output lines using the final values of `ctor_count` and `copy_count`.
7. For N = 0 the loop body never executes; for N = 5 the copy count is 6 (1 from step 4 + 5 from the loop).

exercises.title: Point Factory with Elision Diagnostics
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Point` class and a free function `midpoint(Point a, Point b)` so the program reads two 2-D points, constructs the midpoint via the factory function, prints diagnostics logged during construction and copying, then prints the result.

## Input
- Line 1: two integers `x1 y1` for the first point (−1000 ≤ each ≤ 1000).
- Line 2: two integers `x2 y2` for the second point (same range).

## Output
Print lines in this exact order:
1. `Constructed (x1,y1)` — fired when `Point p{x1,y1}` is built.
2. `Constructed (x2,y2)` — fired when `Point p{x2,y2}` is built.
3. `Copied` — fired **twice** because `midpoint` takes both arguments **by value** (two copies from lvalues).
4. `Constructed (mx,my)` — fired inside `midpoint` when the return value is built (mx and my are the integer midpoint coordinates, computed as `(x1+x2)/2` and `(y1+y2)/2` using integer division).
5. `Midpoint: (mx,my)` — the final result printed in `main`.

No other lines. No blank lines.

## Requirements
1. `struct Point` must have `int x` and `int y` fields.
2. The constructor `Point(int x, int y)` must print `Constructed (x,y)` (parentheses, comma, no spaces inside).
3. The copy constructor `Point(const Point&)` must print `Copied` (no coordinates).
4. `Point midpoint(Point a, Point b)` takes **both arguments by value**, computes `Point r{ (a.x+b.x)/2, (a.y+b.y)/2 }`, and returns it. Mandatory copy elision means this `return` fires only the constructor message, not an extra copy.
5. In `main`, construct `p` and `q` from stdin, then call `Point m = midpoint(p, q);` and print `Midpoint: (mx,my)`.
6. The two `Copied` lines appear before the `Constructed` line for the midpoint because the copies happen when arguments are passed.
7. Edge case: when both inputs are identical, midpoint coordinates equal the input coordinates.

test_cases.label: Basic midpoint (sample)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed (0,0)
Constructed (4,6)
Copied
Copied
Constructed (2,3)
Midpoint: (2,3)

test_cases.label: Both points identical
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed (5,5)
Constructed (5,5)
Copied
Copied
Constructed (5,5)
Midpoint: (5,5)

test_cases.label: Negative coordinates with truncating division
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Constructed (-3,-7)
Constructed (2,4)
Copied
Copied
Constructed (-1,-2)
Midpoint: (-1,-2)

lessons.title: Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

exercises.title: Explicit Wallet Constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Wallet` struct so that its single-argument constructor is marked `explicit`, then write a standalone function `add_funds` that accepts a `Wallet` and a `double`, and returns a new `Wallet` whose balance equals the sum.

## Input
- Line 1: a single `double` representing the initial wallet balance (0.0 ≤ value ≤ 10000.0)
- Line 2: a single `double` representing funds to add (0.0 ≤ value ≤ 10000.0)

## Output
- Print exactly two lines:
  - `Initial: X` where X is the initial balance formatted to 2 decimal places
  - `After deposit: Y` where Y is the balance after adding funds, formatted to 2 decimal places

## Requirements
1. `Wallet` must have a single `double` field named `m_balance`.
2. `Wallet`'s constructor `Wallet(double balance)` must be marked `explicit`.
3. Implement `Wallet add_funds(Wallet w, double amount)` that returns a `Wallet` constructed from `w.m_balance + amount`.
4. Inside `main`, create the initial `Wallet` using direct initialization (e.g., `Wallet w{initial};`). The code must **not** compile if you replace that line with copy initialization (`Wallet w = initial;`).
5. The `static_assert` block in the provided starter code must remain: it uses `std::is_constructible` to verify the constructor exists with the right argument type.
6. Output values must use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)`.
7. Edge cases: initial balance of `0.0`, added funds of `0.0`, both at maximum `10000.0`.

## Notes
- You may include `<iostream>`, `<iomanip>`, and `<type_traits>`.
- Do not add any other constructors to `Wallet`.

test_cases.label: Hidden — zero balance and zero deposit

exercises.title: Rectangle with Explicit Square Constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Rectangle` class that stores width and height, a converting constructor that builds a square from a single `double` side length (marked `explicit`), and a two-parameter constructor for non-square rectangles. Then implement a member function `area()` and a non-member function `scale` that returns a new `Rectangle` scaled by a factor.

## Input
- Line 1: two doubles `W` and `H` separated by a space — the width and height of a rectangle (0.1 ≤ W, H ≤ 500.0)
- Line 2: one double `S` — the side length of a square (0.1 ≤ S ≤ 500.0)
- Line 3: one double `F` — the scale factor applied to the square (0.1 ≤ F ≤ 10.0)

## Output
Print exactly three lines:
- `Rectangle area: A` where A is `W * H`, formatted to 2 decimal places
- `Square area: B` where B is `S * S`, formatted to 2 decimal places
- `Scaled square area: C` where C is `(S * F) * (S * F)`, formatted to 2 decimal places

## Requirements
1. `Rectangle` must have two `double` fields: `m_width` and `m_height`.
2. Provide a two-parameter constructor `Rectangle(double width, double height)` that initializes both fields.
3. Provide a single-parameter constructor `Rectangle(double side)` marked `explicit` that sets both `m_width` and `m_height` to `side` (building a square).
4. Implement `double area() const` as a member function returning `m_width * m_height`.
5. Implement a non-member function `Rectangle scale(Rectangle r, double factor)` that returns a new `Rectangle` constructed from `r.m_width * factor` and `r.m_height * factor` using the two-parameter constructor.
6. In `main`, the square must be created with direct initialization (`Rectangle sq{S};`). The code must **not** compile if copy initialization (`Rectangle sq = S;`) is used instead.
7. The call `scale(sq, F)` must compile and produce the scaled square's area.
8. Edge cases: W == H (already a square shape via two-parameter constructor), F == 1.0 (no change), minimum values.

## Notes
- You may include `<iostream>` and `<iomanip>`.
- Do not remove or modify the `static_assert` in the starter code.

test_cases.label: Sample — typical rectangle, square, and scale
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Rectangle area: 20.00
Square area: 9.00
Scaled square area: 36.00

lessons.title: Constexpr aggregates and classes

exercises.title: Constexpr Rectangle Aggregate
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Rectangle` struct and a `constexpr` helper function `area()` so that rectangle dimensions read from stdin are validated at compile time using `static_assert`, and runtime dimensions are used to print area and perimeter.

## Input
- Line 1: two positive integers `W` and `H` (1 ≤ W, H ≤ 1000) separated by a space, representing the width and height of a rectangle.

## Output
- Print exactly two lines:
  - `Area: X` where X is W * H
  - `Perimeter: Y` where Y is 2 * (W + H)
- No trailing blank lines. No extra spaces.

## Requirements
1. Define `Rectangle` as a **constexpr aggregate** with two `int` fields named `width` and `height` (in that order).
2. Define a **`constexpr` free function** `area(Rectangle r)` that returns `r.width * r.height` as an `int`.
3. Define a **`constexpr` free function** `perimeter(Rectangle r)` that returns `2 * (r.width + r.height)` as an `int`.
4. Inside `main`, include a `static_assert` that verifies `area(Rectangle{3, 4}) == 12`. This must compile.
5. Include a `static_assert` that verifies `perimeter(Rectangle{3, 4}) == 14`. This must compile.
6. Read `W` and `H` from stdin, construct a (non-constexpr) `Rectangle` at runtime, and print `Area:` and `Perimeter:` as described.
7. Edge cases covered by hidden tests: W == H (square), W or H == 1, W and H both == 1000.

exercises.title: Constexpr Temperature Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Temperature` class with a `constexpr` constructor and `constexpr` member functions, then read a temperature value and scale from stdin and print converted results.

## Input
- Line 1: a floating-point number `T` (-273.15 ≤ T ≤ 10000.0) and a single character `S` ('C' for Celsius, 'F' for Fahrenheit, 'K' for Kelvin), separated by a space.
- `T` is always provided in the scale indicated by `S`.

## Output
- Print exactly three lines showing the temperature in all three scales in this order:
  - `Celsius: X`
  - `Fahrenheit: X`
  - `Kelvin: X`
- Each value X is rounded to **2 decimal places** using `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(2)`.

## Requirements
1. Define a class `Temperature` with a single `double` private data member `m_celsius`.
2. The class must have a **`constexpr` constructor** that accepts a `double` representing degrees Celsius and stores it in `m_celsius`.
3. Define three **`constexpr` member functions** (all `const`):
   - `celsius()` returns `m_celsius`.
   - `fahrenheit()` returns `m_celsius * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0`.
   - `kelvin()` returns `m_celsius + 273.15`.
4. Define **three `static constexpr` factory functions** (class members) that each accept a `double` and return a `Temperature`:
   - `fromCelsius(double c)` returns `Temperature{c}`.
   - `fromFahrenheit(double f)` returns `Temperature{(f - 32.0) * 5.0 / 9.0}`.
   - `fromKelvin(double k)` returns `Temperature{k - 273.15}`.
5. Inside `main`, include a `static_assert` that verifies `Temperature::fromCelsius(0.0).fahrenheit() == 32.0`.
6. Include a `static_assert` that verifies `Temperature::fromFahrenheit(212.0).celsius() == 100.0`.
7. Read `T` and `S` from stdin, construct a `Temperature` using the matching factory, then print the three lines.
8. Edge cases covered by hidden tests: absolute zero (-273.15 K), 0°C (freezing), 100°C (boiling), input given in Fahrenheit.

exercises.title: Chainable Counter Builder
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Counter` struct so that its member functions return `*this` by reference, enabling fluent method chaining.

## Input
- Line 1: three integers `a`, `b`, `c` separated by spaces.
  - `a`: the amount to pass to `add` (−1000 ≤ a ≤ 1000)
  - `b`: the amount to pass to `subtract` (−1000 ≤ b ≤ 1000)
  - `c`: the factor to pass to `multiply` (−100 ≤ c ≤ 100)

## Output
Print exactly one line in the format `Value: X` where `X` is the final integer value of the counter after the chain `add(a).subtract(b).multiply(c)` is applied to a counter starting at 0.

## Requirements
1. Define `Counter` with a single `int` data member named `value`, initialized to `0`.
2. Implement `Counter& add(int n)` — increases `value` by `n` and returns `*this`.
3. Implement `Counter& subtract(int n)` — decreases `value` by `n` and returns `*this`.
4. Implement `Counter& multiply(int n)` — multiplies `value` by `n` and returns `*this`.
5. Implement `void print() const` — prints `Value: ` followed by `value` and a newline.
6. `main` must call the chain as a single expression: `c.add(a).subtract(b).multiply(c_val).print();`
7. The hidden tests cover: `c = 0` (multiply wipes result), `a` and `b` equal (value is 0 before multiply), and large values near the stated limits.

test_cases.label: hidden — multiply by zero wipes result
hidden — add equals subtract so value is zero before multiply

exercises.title: Counter with Fluent Interface
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a `Counter` class that supports method chaining by returning `*this` from each mutating method.

## Requirements

- Data member: `int value` initialized to `0`.
- Methods:
  - `Counter& increment()` — adds 1 to `value`, returns `*this`.
  - `Counter& decrement()` — subtracts 1 from `value`, returns `*this`.
  - `Counter& add(int n)` — adds `n` to `value`, returns `*this`.
  - `Counter& reset()` — sets `value` to `0`, returns `*this`.
  - `void print() const` — prints `value` followed by a newline.
- All chaining methods must return `*this` by reference.

## Input

No stdin. The `main` function is pre-written and tests a fixed chain of calls.

## Output

The exact output of the chained calls provided in `main`.

test_cases.label: Three increments then add/decrement then reset/add
Reset brings value back to zero

exercises.title: Chainable String Formatter with const Print
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `TextLine` struct whose mutable methods build up a string via method chaining and whose `const` print method is callable at the end of the chain.

## Input
- Line 1: a single non-empty word (no spaces, length 1–20) — the initial text.
- Line 2: a single non-empty word — text to append after a space.
- Line 3: a single character `'U'` or `'L'` indicating whether to convert the accumulated text to upper-case (`'U'`) or lower-case (`'L'`).

## Output
Print exactly two lines:
1. `Before: ` followed by the text after `append` but before the case conversion.
2. `After: ` followed by the text after the case conversion.

Both lines must be produced by chained calls ending in `printBefore()` and `printAfter()` respectively (see Requirements).

## Requirements
1. Define `TextLine` with a single `std::string` data member named `text`.
2. Implement `TextLine(std::string start)` — constructor that initializes `text` to `start`.
3. Implement `TextLine& append(const std::string& word)` — appends a space and `word` to `text`, returns `*this`.
4. Implement `TextLine& toUpper()` — converts every character in `text` to upper-case using `std::toupper`, returns `*this`.
5. Implement `TextLine& toLower()` — converts every character in `text` to lower-case using `std::tolower`, returns `*this`.
6. Implement `const TextLine& printBefore() const` — prints `Before: ` followed by `text` and a newline, then returns `*this` (as `const TextLine&`).
7. Implement `const TextLine& printAfter() const` — prints `After: ` followed by `text` and a newline, then returns `*this`.
8. `main` must build the chain as one expression: create a `TextLine`, call `append`, call `printBefore`, then call `toUpper` or `toLower`, then call `printAfter` — all chained.
9. Hidden tests cover: single-character inputs, `'L'` conversion on already-lower text, and mixed-case initial words.

## Notes
- You may `#include <cctype>` and `#include <algorithm>` for `std::toupper`/`std::tolower` and `std::transform`.
- `printBefore` must return `const TextLine&` so that `toUpper`/`toLower` can be chained after it — those are non-const methods on a non-const object; the chain from `main` is on a non-const temporary.

test_cases.label: sample — upper-case conversion
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Before: Hello World
After: HELLO WORLD

test_cases.label: hidden — lower-case conversion on already mixed text
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Before: GoOdBye MoOn
After: goodbye moon

test_cases.label: hidden — single-character words upper-case
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Before: a b
After: A B

exercises.title: String Builder with History
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a `TextBuilder` class that constructs a string via chained calls and tracks how many operations were applied. This exercise combines the `this` pointer / fluent interface pattern with a running operation count.

## Requirements

- Data members: `std::string text` (starts empty) and `int ops` (operation count, starts at `0`).
- Methods (each increments `ops` by 1 and returns `*this` by reference):
  - `TextBuilder& append(const std::string& s)` — appends `s` to `text`.
  - `TextBuilder& prepend(const std::string& s)` — inserts `s` at the front of `text`.
  - `TextBuilder& toUpper()` — converts every character in `text` to uppercase.
- `void print() const` — prints `text` on one line, then `ops` on the next line.

## Input

Three lines:
1. A word to append first.
2. A word to prepend.
3. A word to append second.

## Output

After calling `.append(first).prepend(second_word).append(third_word).toUpper().print()` (in that order), print:
- Line 1: the final `text` in uppercase.
- Line 2: the total number of ops (always `4` for the fixed chain).

## Example

Input:
```
world
hello 
!
```
Output:
```
HELLO WORLD!
4
```

lessons.title: Classes and header files

exercises.title: Inline vs Out-of-Line: Annotate a Rectangle Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Complete a single-file `Rectangle` class whose member functions are deliberately split into inline (defined inside the class body) and out-of-line (defined after the class body, with `inline` keyword) forms, and answer a short classification question via output.

## Input

Line 1: two space-separated integers `W` and `H` (1 ≤ W, H ≤ 1000) — the width and height of the rectangle.

## Output

Print exactly three lines:
```
Area: <W*H>
Perimeter: <2*(W+H)>
Is square: <yes|no>
```

No trailing spaces. Newline after each line.

## Requirements

1. Define a class `Rectangle` with private members `m_width` and `m_height` (both `int`).
2. The constructor `Rectangle(int w, int h)` must be defined **outside** the class body using `inline`, taking two `int` parameters and initializing members via a member-initializer list.
3. `int area() const` must be defined **inside** the class body (implicitly inline).
4. `int perimeter() const` must be defined **outside** the class body using `inline`.
5. `bool isSquare() const` must be defined **outside** the class body using `inline`.
6. `main()` reads W and H, constructs a `Rectangle`, and prints the three output lines exactly as shown.
7. Edge case: W == H should print `Is square: yes`; W != H prints `Is square: no`.

## Notes
- Keep everything in one `.cpp` file — the exercise tests your understanding of *where* definitions go and the `inline` keyword, not actual multi-file compilation.

exercises.title: Chainable Temperature Logger with Header-Style Separation
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Complete a `TempLog` class that stores a sequence of temperature readings and supports method chaining (from the prior lesson on `this` and member function chaining), with declarations and out-of-line definitions separated as they would be across a `.h`/`.cpp` pair — all in one file.

## Input

Line 1: integer `N` (1 ≤ N ≤ 100) — the number of readings.
Lines 2..N+1: one floating-point temperature per line (Celsius, any real number).

## Output

Print exactly three lines:
```
Count: <N>
Min: <min value, 1 decimal place>
Max: <max value, 1 decimal place>
```

No trailing spaces. Newline after each line.

## Requirements

1. Define a class `TempLog` with a private `std::vector<double>` member `m_readings`.
2. `add(double t)` must return `TempLog&` so calls can be chained (e.g. `log.add(1.0).add(2.0)`).
3. `int count() const` returns the number of readings stored.
4. `double minTemp() const` returns the minimum reading.
5. `double maxTemp() const` returns the maximum reading.
6. All five member functions must be **declared** inside the class body and **defined outside** the class body (without `inline` — as they would appear in a `.cpp` file).
7. `main()` reads `N`, then uses a chaining loop or individual `add` calls to store readings, then prints the three lines formatted to 1 decimal place using `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)`.
8. Edge case: N == 1 means min and max are the same value.

## Notes
- `#include <vector>`, `#include <algorithm>`, and `#include <iomanip>` are permitted.

exercises.title: Deck of cards with nested suit enum and method chaining
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Combine a nested `enum class` (this lesson) with member function chaining (`this` pointer, from *The hidden `this` pointer and member function chaining*).

## Task

Implement a `Card` class that:

- Has a `public` nested `enum class Suit` with values `Clubs`, `Diamonds`, `Hearts`, `Spades` (in that order, 0-3).
- Stores a `Suit` and a `rank` (integer 1-13, where 1 = Ace, 11 = Jack, 12 = Queen, 13 = King).
- Provides `Card& setSuit(Suit s)` and `Card& setRank(int r)` — both return `*this` to allow chaining.
- Provides `std::string suitName() const` returning `"Clubs"`, `"Diamonds"`, `"Hearts"`, or `"Spades"`.
- Provides `std::string rankName() const` returning `"Ace"`, `"2"` through `"10"`, `"Jack"`, `"Queen"`, or `"King"`.
- Provides `void print() const` that prints `"<rankName> of <suitName>"` followed by a newline.

## Input

Two integers on one line: `suitIndex` (0-3) and `rank` (1-13).

## Output

Print the card description, e.g. `Ace of Spades`.

## Requirements

- `main()` must use chaining: `card.setSuit(...).setRank(...)`.

- Compile cleanly with `g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

lessons.title: Introduction to destructors
lessons.summary_md:
## Introduction to Destructors

A **destructor** is a special member function that runs automatically when an object's lifetime ends. Its job is to release resources the object owns — heap memory, file handles, network sockets, locks, or anything else that needs cleanup.

### Syntax

A destructor has the same name as the class, prefixed with a tilde (`~`), takes no parameters, and returns nothing:

```cpp
class Buffer {
    int* data{};
public:
    explicit Buffer(int size) : data{ new int[size] } {}
    ~Buffer() { delete[] data; }  // destructor — runs when Buffer goes out of scope
};

int main() {
    Buffer b{ 4 };  // constructor runs here
}   // destructor runs here automatically
```

### When destructors run

- **Stack objects:** when the variable goes out of scope (end of block, function return, or exception unwind).
- **Heap objects:** when `delete` (or `delete[]`) is called on the pointer.
- **Members and bases:** a class's destructor body runs first, then member destructors run in **reverse declaration order**, then base-class destructors run. This is the mirror image of construction order.
- **Static and global objects:** at program exit, in reverse construction order.

### RAII — the key idea

Destructors are the foundation of **Resource Acquisition Is Initialization (RAII)**: acquire a resource in the constructor, release it in the destructor. Because destructors run unconditionally (including during exception unwinding), RAII guarantees cleanup even when errors occur. The standard library is built entirely on this pattern — `std::vector`, `std::string`, `std::fstream`, `std::unique_ptr`, and `std::lock_guard` all use destructors to free their resources.

### Rules and limits

- Only one destructor per class. No overloading.
- A destructor cannot be `const`, `volatile`, or `static`.
- If you do not declare one, the compiler generates a defaulted destructor that calls member/base destructors and does nothing else. This is correct for classes that own no raw resources.
- If a class manages a raw resource, follow the **Rule of Five**: if you write a destructor, you almost certainly also need a copy constructor, copy-assignment operator, move constructor, and move-assignment operator (or `= delete` them).

### Relation to earlier material

From *Classes and header files*, remember that the destructor definition typically lives in the `.cpp` file alongside other member function definitions. From *The hidden `this` pointer and member function chaining*, recall that `this` is valid inside a destructor body — though chaining from a destructor is rarely useful.

exercises.title: Track object lifetimes with a destructor

exercises.title: RAII integer array wrapper
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Apply RAII: manage a heap-allocated integer array using a constructor and destructor, then expose a simple interface to sum its elements.

This combines destructors (this lesson) with class design patterns from *Classes and header files* and the member function style from *The hidden `this` pointer and member function chaining*.

## Task

Complete the `IntArray` class so that:

- The constructor takes a `size` (int) and a `fill` value (int, default `0`), allocates a `size`-element `int` array on the heap, and fills every element with `fill`.
- The destructor frees the array with `delete[]`.
- `sum()` returns the sum of all elements as an `int`.
- `set(int index, int value)` sets `data[index] = value` (no bounds checking required).
- `main` reads `size` and `fill` from stdin, then reads `size` replacement values, calls `set` for each, and prints the final `sum()`.

## Input

Line 1: two integers `size` and `fill` (space-separated).  
Line 2: `size` integers — new values to store (one per element, space-separated).

## Output

A single integer: the sum of the array after all `set` calls, followed by a newline.

### Sample

**Input:**
```
4 0
1 2 3 4
```
**Output:**
```
10
```

## Requirements

- Use `new int[size]` in the constructor and `delete[] data` in the destructor.
- Do not use `std::vector` or any standard container inside `IntArray`.
- The program must not leak memory (destructor must fire exactly once).
- Compile cleanly with `g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

test_cases.label: 3-element array with non-zero fill, partial override

lessons.title: Class templates with member functions

exercises.title: Generic Stack class template
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a `Stack<T>` class template that stores elements in a fixed-capacity array and exposes push, pop, and top operations.

## Requirements

- Template parameter `T` is the element type.
- A second non-type template parameter `std::size_t N` sets the maximum capacity (default `16`).
- Member functions:
  - `void push(const T& value)` — adds an element; does nothing if full.
  - `void pop()` — removes the top element; does nothing if empty.
  - `T top() const` — returns the top element (assume non-empty when called).
  - `bool empty() const` — returns `true` when no elements are stored.
  - `std::size_t size() const` — returns the number of stored elements.
- All member functions must be defined **inside** the class template body.
- Compile with `g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

## Input

A single line of space-separated integers, terminated by `0` (the `0` is a sentinel and must NOT be pushed). Push each non-zero integer onto a `Stack<int>` and then print elements from top to bottom by repeatedly printing `top()` and calling `pop()`.

## Output

One integer per line, from top to bottom (i.e. reverse insertion order).

exercises.title: Pair template with chained comparisons
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Combine the `Pair<T, U>` class template from this lesson with the **member function chaining** pattern from *The hidden this pointer and member function chaining* to build a chainable, two-type pair that can report its own minimum and maximum.

## Requirements

- Define `Pair<T, U>` with two separate template parameters so the two values can differ in type.
- Public data members `first` (type `T`) and `second` (type `U`).
- A constructor that takes `(T a, U b)` and initialises both members.
- Member functions defined **outside** the class body (with the full `template <typename T, typename U>` header):
  - `Pair<T,U>& setFirst(T v)` — assigns `first`, returns `*this`.
  - `Pair<T,U>& setSecond(U v)` — assigns `second`, returns `*this`.
- A free function template:
  ```cpp
  template <typename T>
  T pairMin(const Pair<T,T>& p);
  template <typename T>
  T pairMax(const Pair<T,T>& p);
  ```
  These only compile when both members share the same type `T`.

## Input

Two lines, each containing two integers separated by a space. For each line, construct a `Pair<int,int>`, then use the chained setters to overwrite both values with the numbers read, and finally print `min` and `max` on one line separated by a space.

## Output

Two lines, each `<min> <max>` for the corresponding input pair.

exercises.title: Unique ID Generator with Static Counter
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Complete the `Ticket` class so each constructed object receives a unique, monotonically-increasing integer ID starting from 1. Use a static member variable as the ID source. Then use the class in a program that reads ticket requests and reports each ticket's ID and the total ever issued.

Print N lines, one per ticket in creation order:
```
Ticket ID: X
```
Then print one final line:
```
Total issued: N
```

## Requirements

1. `Ticket` must have an `inline static int s_next_id = 1;` member.
2. `Ticket` must have a non-static `int m_id` member set in the constructor.
3. The constructor must assign `m_id = s_next_id` then increment `s_next_id`.
4. Provide a `const` getter `int id() const` that returns `m_id`.
5. After constructing all N tickets and printing their IDs, print total tickets issued using `Ticket::s_next_id - 1`.
6. Edge case: N = 1 prints exactly `Ticket ID: 1` followed by `Total issued: 1`.
7. Edge case: N = 50 — the last ticket has ID 50 and total is 50.

lessons.title: Static member functions

exercises.title: Instance Counter
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a class `Widget` that tracks how many `Widget` objects are currently alive using a static member variable and a static member function.

## Requirements

- `Widget` has a private static `int` named `s_count` initialised to `0`.
- The default constructor increments `s_count`; the destructor decrements it.
- A public static member function `count()` returns the current count.
- `main` reads a single integer `n` from stdin, creates `n` `Widget` objects in a `std::vector`, prints the live count, then clears the vector and prints the count again.

## Input

A single integer `n` (1 ≤ n ≤ 100).

## Output

Two lines:
```
Alive: <n>
Alive: 0
```

exercises.title: Widget Factory Counter
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Widget` class so that a static member function tracks how many `Widget` objects have been created in total (including destroyed ones) and how many are currently alive.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 20), the number of commands.
- Each of the next N lines is one of:
  - `create` — construct a new Widget and push it onto an internal list.
  - `destroy` — destroy the most recently created Widget still alive (pop from the list). Guaranteed at least one Widget is alive when this command appears.
  - `report` — print the current statistics (see Output).

## Output
For each `report` command, print exactly one line in the format:
```
created=C alive=A
```
where `C` is the total number of Widgets ever constructed and `A` is the number currently alive. No extra blank lines.

## Requirements
1. `Widget` must have a `static int s_created` member that increments in the constructor and never decrements.
2. `Widget` must have a `static int s_alive` member that increments in the constructor and decrements in the destructor.
3. Provide `static int getCreated()` and `static int getAlive()` — both static member functions that return the respective static members.
4. `main()` must call `Widget::getCreated()` and `Widget::getAlive()` (not any instance method) when handling `report`.
5. The hidden tests include a `report` as the very first command (0 created, 0 alive) and a `report` immediately after all widgets are destroyed.

test_cases.label: sample — mixed creates, destroy, and reports

create
create
report
create
destroy
report
destroy

test_cases.label: hidden — report before any widget is created
hidden — report after all widgets destroyed
test_cases.stdin:

create
create
create
report
destroy
destroy
destroy
report

exercises.title: Temperature Factory
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Combine static member functions (this lesson) with class templates (from **Class templates with member functions**) to build a `Temperature<T>` class whose instances can only be created through named factory functions.

## Requirements

- `Temperature<T>` stores a single `T m_celsius` value (private).
- The constructor is **private**.
- Two public static factory functions:
  - `Temperature<T> fromCelsius(T c)` — wraps `c` directly.
  - `Temperature<T> fromFahrenheit(T f)` — converts `f` to Celsius: `(f - 32) * 5 / 9`.
- A public member function `T celsius() const` returns the stored value.
- A public member function `T fahrenheit() const` returns the value converted back: `m_celsius * 9 / 5 + 32`.
- `main` reads a mode character (`C` or `F`) and a value, constructs a `Temperature<double>` via the appropriate factory, then prints both representations on separate lines rounded to one decimal place.

## Input

A character (`C` or `F`) followed by a floating-point number.

## Output

```
Celsius: <value>
Fahrenheit: <value>
```

All values printed to exactly one decimal place.

exercises.title: Temperature Log with Static Summary
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `TempLog` class, which records individual temperature readings as non-static data and uses static members plus static member functions to maintain a running summary across all readings ever recorded.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 50), the number of readings.
- Each of the next N lines contains one floating-point temperature value (−100.0 ≤ T ≤ 100.0).

## Output
Print exactly three lines:
```
Count: C
Sum: S
Average: A
```
where:
- `C` is the total number of readings (integer).
- `S` is the sum of all readings, printed with exactly 2 decimal places.
- `A` is the average of all readings, printed with exactly 2 decimal places.

## Requirements
1. `TempLog` must store each individual temperature in a non-static `double m_temp` member set by its constructor.
2. `TempLog` must have `static int s_count` and `static double s_sum` members; both are updated inside the constructor.
3. Provide three static member functions: `getCount()` returning `int`, `getSum()` returning `double`, and `getAverage()` returning `double` (returns `s_sum / s_count`; guaranteed N ≥ 1 so no division by zero).
4. `main()` must create one `TempLog` object per reading and call the three static member functions via the class-scope operator (`TempLog::getCount()`, etc.) to produce the output.
5. The hidden tests include all identical values (verify average equals that value) and a mix of positive and negative readings whose sum is 0.

lessons.title: Friend non-member functions

exercises.title: Friend operator<< and operator+ for a Distance Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Distance` class by implementing two friend non-member functions: `operator+` and `operator<<`, so that two distances can be added and printed.

## Input
- Line 1: two space-separated non-negative integers representing meters and centimeters of the first distance (0 ≤ meters ≤ 1000, 0 ≤ centimeters ≤ 99)
- Line 2: two space-separated non-negative integers representing meters and centimeters of the second distance (same ranges)

## Output
- Print exactly three lines:
  1. `d1: X m Y cm` — the first distance
  2. `d2: X m Y cm` — the second distance
  3. `sum: X m Y cm` — the result of adding the two distances
- Centimeters are normalized: every 100 cm carries over into 1 m. So if the total centimeters is 130, print 1 m more and 30 cm.
- No trailing spaces. Each line ends with a newline.

## Requirements
1. `Distance` must store `m_meters` (int) and `m_centimeters` (int) as private members.
2. Declare and define `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const Distance& d)` — it must print exactly `X m Y cm` with a single space around each token.
3. Declare and define `friend Distance operator+(const Distance& a, const Distance& b)` — it must normalize the result so `m_centimeters` is in [0, 99].
4. The constructor `Distance(int meters, int centimeters)` is provided; do not change it.
5. Neither friend function may access a getter — they must read `m_meters` and `m_centimeters` directly (that is the point of friendship).
6. Edge case: input where combined centimeters equal exactly 100 (carry of exactly 1 m, 0 cm remaining).

sum: 4 m 0 cm

test_cases.label: Hidden — no carry, zeros
test_cases.expected_stdout:

d2: 5 m 30 cm
sum: 5 m 30 cm


- `Box` must store `length`, `width`, `height` as **private** `int` members.
- `operator<<` must be a **friend non-member** function — do not add any public getters.

- Output format: `Box(l=L, w=W, h=H)` followed by a newline.

exercises.title: Friend operator== and operator<< for a Rectangle Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the `Rectangle` class by implementing three friend non-member functions — `operator<<`, `operator==`, and `operator!=` — and use them inside `main` to compare two rectangles read from stdin.

## Input
- Line 1: two space-separated positive integers `W H` — width and height of the first rectangle (1 ≤ W, H ≤ 500)
- Line 2: two space-separated positive integers `W H` — width and height of the second rectangle (same ranges)

## Output
Print exactly four lines:
1. `r1: W x H` — first rectangle
2. `r2: W x H` — second rectangle
3. `area r1: A` — area of the first rectangle (integer)
4. Either `r1 == r2` if the rectangles are equal, or `r1 != r2` if they are not

Two rectangles are **equal** when they have the same width **and** the same height (order matters — 3×5 ≠ 5×3).

## Requirements
1. `Rectangle` must store `m_width` (int) and `m_height` (int) as private members.
2. Provide a constructor `Rectangle(int width, int height)` and a private member function `area() const` that returns `m_width * m_height`.
3. Declare and define `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const Rectangle& r)` — prints `W x H` (space-x-space between the two numbers).
4. Declare and define `friend bool operator==(const Rectangle& a, const Rectangle& b)` — returns true when both dimensions match.
5. Declare and define `friend bool operator!=(const Rectangle& a, const Rectangle& b)` — must be implemented by calling `operator==` (i.e. `return !(a == b);`).
6. The line `area r1: A` must call the private `area()` member function from within `operator<<` — to do so, `operator<<` must be a friend.
7. Edge cases: equal rectangles (same W and H), swapped dimensions (W and H swapped between r1 and r2).

test_cases.label: Sample — unequal rectangles
test_cases.expected_stdout:
r1: 4 x 6
r2: 3 x 8
area r1: 24
r1 != r2

test_cases.label: Hidden — equal rectangles
test_cases.expected_stdout:
r1: 7 x 5
r2: 7 x 5
area r1: 35
r1 == r2

exercises.title: Temperature class with friend operator<< and static conversion helper
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Combine friend non-member functions (this lesson) with static member functions (from **Static member functions**) to build a `Temperature` class.

## Input

Two lines. Each line has a number and a unit letter (`C` for Celsius, `F` for Fahrenheit). Example:
```
100 C
32 F
```

## Output

Print each temperature using `operator<<` in the format `T°C` or `T°F` (with the degree sign `\u00b0` — use `\u00b0` in source or just the character `°`). Then print which is hotter, or "Equal" if they are the same in Celsius. Comparison is done in Celsius rounded to one decimal place for display, but use the raw double for comparison.

```
100°C
32°F
Hotter: 100°C
```

If the second is hotter:
```
0°C
100°F
Hotter: 100°F
```

If equal:
```
0°C
32°F
Equal
```

## Requirements

- `Temperature` stores value as `double` and unit as `char` — both **private**.
- `static double toCelsius(double val, char unit)` — public static member function (converts F to C; returns C unchanged).
- `operator<<` is a **friend non-member** function; do not add public getters.
- `hotter(const Temperature&, const Temperature&)` is a **friend non-member** function that returns a `const Temperature*` pointing to the hotter one, or `nullptr` if equal. Use `toCelsius` inside it via the class name.
- Degree sign: use the UTF-8 character `°` directly in your string literals.
- Compile cleanly with `g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

  - A **friend member function** declaration: `friend void Auditor::report(const BankAccount&)`.
- Define a class `Auditor` (fully defined **before** `BankAccount` so the friend declaration is valid — use a forward declaration of `BankAccount` inside `Auditor` if needed) with:
  - A public member function `void report(const BankAccount& acct)` that prints one line in the format shown below, accessing `balance` and `s_totalDeposited` directly.
- `main` reads a number of operations. Each operation is one of:
  - `D amount` — deposit `amount` into the current account.
  - `R` — call `auditor.report(account)`.
- There is exactly one `BankAccount` (initial balance `0.0`) and one `Auditor`.

## Input

First line: integer `N` (number of operations).
Next `N` lines: each is `D <amount>` or `R`.

## Output

```
Balance: 50.00, TotalDeposited: 75.00
```
(two decimal places, using actual values)

- `s_totalDeposited` must be a static member of `BankAccount`, not a global variable.

exercises.title: Builder with ref-qualified build(): lvalue copies, rvalue moves
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Combine ref qualifiers with the idea of a simple builder class (using a constructor and member data, as seen in **The hidden `this` pointer**) to produce a class that is cheap to consume when temporary and safe to reuse when an lvalue.

## Task

Complete the `QueryBuilder` class that accumulates a query string.

- `QueryBuilder& append(std::string_view part)` — appends `part` followed by a space to the internal buffer. Returns `*this` to allow chaining.
- `std::string build() const &` — returns a copy of the accumulated string (trims the trailing space).
- `std::string build() &&` — moves the accumulated string out and returns it (also trims the trailing space).

To trim the trailing space: if the string is non-empty and ends with `' '`, call `.pop_back()` before returning.

## Input

Read lines from stdin until EOF. Each line is appended to the builder. Then call `build()` and print the result, followed by a newline.

## Output

A single line: all input tokens joined by single spaces.

### Example

**Input:**
```
SELECT *
FROM users
WHERE id = 1
```

**Output:**
```
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1
```

## Requirements

- Both `build()` overloads must carry correct ref qualifiers.
- The `&&` overload must use `std::move` on the internal buffer.
- `append` must return `*this` by reference.
- Compile cleanly with `-std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

exercises.title: Generic Accumulator with Friend and Chaining
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement a class template `Accum<T>` that accumulates values via method chaining, and a friend non-member function `printAccum` that prints its internal total.

## Requirements

- `Accum<T>` stores a running total `m_total` of type `T`, initialized to `T{}`.
- `Accum<T>& add(T val) &` adds `val` to `m_total` and returns `*this` (enabling chaining).
- `Accum<T>& scale(T factor) &` multiplies `m_total` by `factor` and returns `*this`.
- A `friend` non-member function `void printAccum(const Accum<T>& a)` prints `m_total` followed by a newline. It must access `m_total` directly (it is private).
- In `main`, read integers from stdin: first `n`, then `n` values to add, then a scale factor. Use chaining to add all values then scale. Call `printAccum` to print the result. Repeat for a second `Accum<double>` with the same input reinterpreted as doubles.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `n`
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (values to add)
- Line 3: an integer scale factor

## Output

- Line 1: the integer result
- Line 2: the double result (same numeric value, printed with default stream precision)

## Example

Input:
```
3
2 3 5
4
```

Output:
```
40
40
```

(2+3+5=10, 10*4=40)

lessons.title: Introduction to containers and arrays
lessons.summary_md:
## Introduction to Containers and Arrays

A **container** is an object that holds a collection of other objects. C++ provides several standard containers, each with different performance characteristics and use cases. The three you'll encounter most often are:

- **C-style arrays** (`int arr[5]`) — fixed-size, stack-allocated, no bounds checking, inherited from C. Avoid in modern C++.
- **`std::array<T, N>`** — fixed-size, stack-allocated, same performance as a C-style array but with safety features like `.size()` and iterator support. Prefer over C-style arrays when the size is known at compile time.
- **`std::vector<T>`** — dynamically-sized, heap-allocated, resizes automatically. The default container for most tasks in modern C++.

All three are **sequential containers**: elements are stored in a linear order and can be accessed by integer index.

### Key properties every container tracks

- **Size** — how many elements are currently stored
- **Capacity** (vector only) — how many elements can fit before reallocation
- **Element type** — all elements share the same type

### Why prefer `std::vector` by default

`std::vector` manages its own memory, so you never have to worry about allocation or deallocation. When you add an element past the current capacity, the vector allocates a larger block, copies existing elements, and releases the old block automatically. This lets you write code that focuses on logic rather than memory management.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

int main() {
    std::vector<int> scores { 88, 92, 75, 100, 67 };

    for (int s : scores)
        std::cout << s << ' ';
    std::cout << '\n';

    std::cout << "Count: " << scores.size() << '\n';
    return 0;
}
```

Output:
```
88 92 75 100 67 
Count: 5
```

### Container choice rules of thumb

- Size known at compile time and small: use `std::array<T, N>`
- Size unknown or changes at runtime: use `std::vector<T>`
- Never use raw C-style arrays in new code — they decay to pointers and lose size information

The rest of this chapter focuses on `std::vector` because it covers the widest range of real-world scenarios and introduces the dynamic-allocation concepts every C++ programmer needs.

lessons.title: Introduction to std::vector and list constructors
lessons.summary_md:
## `std::vector` and List Constructors

`std::vector` is the standard dynamic array. It manages heap-allocated storage automatically, growing as needed. You declare it with `#include <vector>`.

### Construction styles

**Default construction** creates an empty vector:
```cpp
std::vector<int> v; // size 0, no heap allocation yet
```

**Size constructor** creates a vector of _n_ elements, each value-initialized (zero for numeric types, default-constructed for class types):
```cpp
std::vector<double> scores(5); // five 0.0 values
```

**Size + value constructor** fills every element with a specific value:
```cpp
std::vector<int> filled(4, 7); // {7, 7, 7, 7}
```

**List initialization** (`{}` braces) lets you specify every element directly. This is the most readable form when you know the elements at compile time:
```cpp
std::vector<std::string> names{"Alice", "Bob", "Carol"};
```

A critical disambiguation: `std::vector<int> a(3)` creates three zeros; `std::vector<int> b{3}` creates one element whose value is `3`. The brace form prefers the `std::initializer_list` constructor when the types match.

**CTAD (Class Template Argument Deduction, C++17)** lets the compiler deduce the element type from an initializer list, so you can write:
```cpp
std::vector v{1.0, 2.5, 3.14}; // deduced as std::vector<double>
```
CTAD only works with list initialization, not the size constructors.

### Key members

- `.size()` — number of elements (returns `size_t`, an unsigned type)
- `.empty()` — true if size is zero
- `operator[]` — unchecked element access
- `.at(i)` — bounds-checked access, throws `std::out_of_range`

### A complete example

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

int main() {
    std::vector<int> counts(3, 0);   // {0, 0, 0}
    std::vector<int> primes{2, 3, 5, 7};

    for (int p : primes)
        std::cout << p << ' ';
    std::cout << '\n';

    std::cout << "counts size: " << counts.size() << '\n';
    return 0;
}
```

Outputs:
```
2 3 5 7
counts size: 3
```

### Prefer `{}` for element lists, `()` for size/count

When constructing with a known set of values use brace initialization. When you just want _n_ default-initialized slots use the parenthesis form. Mixing them up is a common bug — rely on the rule above rather than trial and error.

lessons.title: Arrays and loops
lessons.summary_md:
## Arrays and Loops

The most common way to work with a `std::vector` is to loop over it. This lesson covers the main traversal patterns you will use in practice.

### Index-based loops

An index-based `for` loop gives you both the element and its position. Because `std::vector::size()` returns `size_t` (an unsigned type), compare your index variable against `std::size()` or cast carefully to avoid signed/unsigned mismatch warnings.

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

int main() {
    std::vector<int> scores { 88, 74, 95, 61, 82 };

    int total { 0 };
    for (std::size_t i { 0 }; i < scores.size(); ++i)
        total += scores[i];

    std::cout << "Average: " << total / static_cast<int>(scores.size()) << '\n';
}
```

### Off-by-one errors

The most common loop bug: iterating one step too far or stopping one step too early.

- Use `i < v.size()` (strict less-than), never `i <= v.size()`.
- The last valid index is `v.size() - 1`. Accessing `v[v.size()]` is undefined behaviour.
- When iterating backwards, start at `v.size() - 1` and stop at `0`. Use a `std::size_t` index carefully here — decrementing an unsigned zero wraps around. A safe pattern is `for (std::size_t i { v.size() }; i-- > 0; )`.

### Range-based for loops

When you do not need the index, a range-`for` eliminates the off-by-one risk entirely:

```cpp
for (int score : scores)
    total += score;
```

Use `const auto&` for elements of non-trivial types to avoid unnecessary copies.

### Accumulation pattern

Initialise an accumulator variable to a neutral value (0 for sums, `INT_MIN` for max) before the loop, update it inside, and use it after. This pattern appears in sum, product, min, max, and count operations.

### Search pattern

Loop over the vector and return (or record) the index as soon as a condition is met. Break early when a match is found — do not continue iterating unnecessarily.

- Return `-1` or use `std::optional<std::size_t>` to signal "not found".
- Linear search is O(n); acceptable for unsorted vectors of moderate size.

### Choosing the right loop

- Need the index or want to modify elements by position: index-based `for`.
- Just reading elements: range-`for`.
- Searching with early exit: index-based `for` with `break` or early `return`.

lessons.title: Array indexing and length using enumerators
lessons.summary_md:
## Array indexing and length using enumerators

Using raw integer literals as array indices (`arr[0]`, `arr[3]`) is error-prone and unreadable. Enumerators solve this by giving each index a meaningful name.

### Unscoped enums as array indices

An unscoped `enum` implicitly converts to `std::size_t`, so its values can index directly into a `std::vector` or array without a cast:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

enum Stat { hp, mp, stamina, count };

int main() {
    std::vector<int> stats(count); // sized to 3
    stats[hp]      = 100;
    stats[mp]      = 50;
    stats[stamina] = 75;
    std::cout << "HP: " << stats[hp] << '\n';
}
```

The sentinel enumerator `count` placed last serves two purposes: it equals the number of elements, so you can size the vector with `std::vector<int>(count)`, and it stays automatically correct when you add or remove enumerators above it.

### Why the sentinel works

- Enumerators start at 0 by default and increment by 1.
- `count` therefore always equals the number of enumerators before it.
- Passing `count` to the vector constructor creates exactly the right number of slots.
- Adding a new stat between existing ones and before `count` automatically resizes the vector — no magic number to update.

### Scoped enums (`enum class`) and the cast problem

`enum class` does not implicitly convert to an integer, so indexing requires `static_cast<std::size_t>(Stat::hp)`. That verbosity defeats the purpose. Two practical remedies:

- Use an unscoped `enum` inside a dedicated namespace to get name-scoping without the cast.
- Write a small helper: `auto idx(Stat s) { return static_cast<std::size_t>(s); }` to centralise the cast.

### Named indices vs. `std::map`

A `std::vector` keyed by enum is a fixed-size, cache-friendly structure with O(1) access. Prefer it over `std::map<Stat, int>` whenever the set of keys is known at compile time and dense (no gaps in the enum values).

### Key takeaways

- Define enumerators starting at 0 with no explicit values to keep indices contiguous.
- Always add a `count` (or `max`, `total`) sentinel as the last enumerator.
- Prefer unscoped enums (possibly in a namespace) when direct indexing is the goal.
- Never mix enumerator-indexed arrays with manual integer indices — pick one convention per array.

lessons.title: std::vector resizing and capacity
lessons.summary_md:
## std::vector Resizing and Capacity

`std::vector` manages two distinct numbers internally: **size** (how many elements currently exist) and **capacity** (how many elements fit in the currently allocated block before a new allocation is needed).

- `v.size()` — number of active elements
- `v.capacity()` — total slots in the current allocation
- `v.resize(n)` — sets size to `n`; value-initializes new elements if growing, destroys trailing elements if shrinking; may reallocate
- `v.reserve(n)` — ensures capacity is at least `n` without changing size or initializing elements
- `v.shrink_to_fit()` — non-binding request to release excess capacity (capacity → size)

### Why capacity matters

Every time a `push_back` exceeds capacity, the vector must allocate a larger block, copy or move all existing elements, then free the old block. This is an O(n) operation. To avoid it happening repeatedly, vectors grow by a **growth factor** (typically 1.5× or 2×). The result is **amortized O(1)** `push_back`: most appends are cheap; occasional expensive reallocations are averaged out.

When you know the final size in advance, call `reserve` first to eliminate all reallocations:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>

int main() {
    std::vector<int> v;
    v.reserve(1000);              // one allocation up front
    for (int i = 0; i < 1000; ++i)
        v.push_back(i * i);
    std::cout << "size=" << v.size()
              << " capacity=" << v.capacity() << '\n';
}
```

### resize vs reserve

- `resize(n)` **creates** elements — after the call, `v[0]` through `v[n-1]` are valid.
- `reserve(n)` **does not** create elements — only raw memory is prepared. Accessing `v[n-1]` after only `reserve` is undefined behaviour.

### Invalidation

Any reallocation invalidates all iterators, pointers, and references into the vector. Code that stores a pointer to `v[0]` before a `push_back` may read freed memory afterwards. `reserve` before appending prevents this.

### Practical rules

- Use `reserve` when you know the count before building the vector (e.g., reading N items from input).
- Prefer `resize` when you need indexed writes rather than `push_back`.
- Call `shrink_to_fit` after large removals when memory footprint matters.
- Never assume a specific capacity value — treat it as an implementation detail.

exercises.title: Reserve and Report
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Practice using `reserve` to pre-allocate capacity and observe the difference between `size` and `capacity`.

## Input

A single integer `n` (1 ≤ n ≤ 10000) on one line.

## Output

Print four lines exactly:
```
before push_back: size=0 capacity=<reserved>
after push_back: size=<n> capacity=<cap>
shrink: size=<n> capacity=<after_shrink>
```
Where `<reserved>` is `n` (from `reserve(n)`), `<cap>` is the capacity after pushing `n` elements (which equals `n` since no reallocation occurred), and `<after_shrink>` is the capacity after calling `shrink_to_fit` (also `n`).

Note: since we `reserve(n)` and then `push_back` exactly `n` elements, no reallocation occurs, so all three capacity values equal `n`.

## Requirements

- Call `v.reserve(n)` before any `push_back`.
- Push the integers 0, 1, 2, …, n-1 with `push_back`.
- Print size and capacity before pushing, after pushing, and after `shrink_to_fit`.
- Do not use `resize`.
